entries.content:
For rotator cuff tendinopathy, physical therapy is the main treatment, with programs designed to be progressive and individualized. Management of rotator cuff tendinopathy
+1

Modality selection
Primary PT components

Mobility work to prevent stiffness; generally aim for full range of motion before strengthening.
Strengthening of the rotator cuff plus scapular stabilizers, with attention to coordination and kinetic chain deficits.
Eccentric exercise can be included; preliminary trials suggest benefit but further study is needed.
Manual therapy can be added; it may decrease pain, and manual therapy plus exercise appears modestly superior to exercise alone short term.
Modalities generally not recommended as routine care

Do not routinely use “electrophysical” adjuncts such as electrical stimulation, phonophoresis, or iontophoresis due to lack of high-quality evidence.
Do not rely on therapeutic ultrasound for tendinopathy; there is no convincing evidence of effectiveness.
Selected adjuncts with limited/conditional evidence

Hyperthermia (microwave-based deep heating) has trial data suggesting short-term benefit versus ultrasound or exercise in supraspinatus tendinopathy, but further research is needed before routine use.

For knee osteoarthritis, the core PT “modality” is ongoing exercise, typically combining low-impact aerobic training and lower-limb strengthening; there is no strong evidence for a single best exercise prescription. Management of mild knee osteoarthritis Management of moderate to severe knee osteoarthritis
+1

Modality selection
Primary

Exercise for all patients.
Preferred exercise mix: low-impact aerobic fitness training plus lower-limb strengthening.
+1
Tai chi and yoga can be used as complementary exercise options.
PT referral use case

Physical therapy referral is often used to optimize the effectiveness of the exercise program and personalize it to impairments and preferences.
Adjunct symptom-modulating modalities

Local heat may have short-term pain benefit but lacks robust trial evidence; some patients may find icing temporarily helpful during flares or swelling.
Modalities to generally avoid

Do not recommend transcutaneous electrical nerve stimulation for knee OA; sham-controlled trials showed no added benefit, and there is evidence of a placebo component.
Shortwave diathermy also showed no added benefit versus sham in a trial within an education and exercise program.

For tendinopathy, the key “modality” is exercise therapy emphasizing slow, progressive, heavy-load exercise, preferred over rest alone and over many passive modalities. Management of tendinopathy

Modality selection
Primary

Progressive heavy-load resistance exercise (eccentric and/or concentric isotonic loading; isometrics may be included but are not clearly superior).
+1
Supportive adjuncts (symptom relief or biomechanics)

Supportive interventions can include stretching, joint mobilization, and appropriate orthoses.
Ice or heat are not important treatments after the acute phase, but can be used as adjuncts for symptom relief in some patients.
Escalation when exercise-based rehab is not successful

Extracorporeal shock wave therapy can be considered as a secondary treatment when exercise therapy alone is not successful, recognizing mixed evidence.
+1
Modalities generally not supported as effective stand-alone treatments

Therapeutic ultrasound lacks definitive evidence for tendinopathy and systematic reviews suggest marginal or no benefit versus placebo or other modalities.

For chronic low back pain, the core “modality” should be regular exercise or movement-based therapy, with other interventions used mainly to support participation and short-term symptom relief.

Suggested modality selection
Foundation

Exercise therapy: recommend participation in a regular exercise program; most exercise types appear similarly effective, so selection is guided by availability and patient interest. Selected exercise modalities
+1
Adjuncts for short-term symptom relief to facilitate active therapy

Superficial heat can be used for short-term symptomatic relief, including during flares.
+1
Selected “passive” therapies (spinal manipulation, acupuncture, massage) may be considered for short-term symptomatic improvement, ideally as a bridge to active therapies.
+1
When psychosocial risk or disability is prominent

Cognitive-behavioral therapy can be added for higher-risk subacute pain or chronic pain with significant functional impairment.
Modalities to generally avoid for chronic low back pain

Do not routinely recommend or refer for transcutaneous electrical nerve stimulation, therapeutic ultrasound, or shortwave diathermy due to little evidence of consistent benefit from randomized trials.

You are asking about the efficacy and typical use cases for microwave and shortwave diathermy, infrared therapy, low- and high-frequency therapeutic ultrasound, and TENS in physical therapy. I am assuming the question is about pain and musculoskeletal rehabilitation in adults.

Is there anything to add or change? 
 

Across chronic pain and rehabilitation contexts, these modalities are generally considered adjunctive options with variable or uncertain benefit, and evidence comparing specific modalities is limited.
+1

Table:Efficacy and use cases (as supported in UpToDate)
Modality	Efficacy signal	Use case framing in UpToDate
Microwave diathermy	Insufficient evidence of effectiveness to recommend for chronic pain.	Not recommended as a chronic pain treatment modality based on insufficient evidence.
Shortwave diathermy	For chronic low back pain, little evidence of consistent benefit from randomized trials.	Listed among therapies not routinely recommended for chronic low back pain.
Infrared therapy	UpToDate content retrieved here did not directly address infrared therapy as a PT pain modality (distinct from “infrared coagulation,” which is a hemorrhoid procedure).	Use case not established from retrieved content.
Therapeutic ultrasound (including low vs high frequency)	Insufficient evidence of effectiveness to recommend for chronic pain. For chronic low back pain, little evidence of consistent benefit. For tendinopathy, definitive evidence is lacking and systematic reviews suggest marginal or no benefit versus placebo or other modalities.
+2
Used as a heat modality; low-frequency ranges can heat deeper tissues. Evidence comparing heat-delivery modalities suggests similar pain relief across methods. Variable evidence for specific musculoskeletal disorders; wearable ultrasound has emerging evidence, strongest for knee osteoarthritis, but requires prolonged daily wear and may be costly with limited reimbursement.
+1
TENS	Uncertain benefit for most chronic pain, may be used as an adjunct. Suggested for a subset with localized pain as part of a broader plan, with tempered expectations; evidence quality is low with mainly short-term benefits and inconclusive long-term efficacy. For chronic low back pain, listed among therapies not routinely recommended due to limited evidence of consistent benefit. Not recommended for knee osteoarthritis; sham-controlled trials showed no added benefit.
+3
Adjunct option some patients may try for localized chronic pain, recognizing heterogeneity of devices and application techniques. May help acute pain when appropriately dosed; evidence does not support use for chronic pain due to trial limitations. In cancer-related pain, evidence is limited and trials/systematic reviews are negative or insufficient, though some patients may benefit in practice. Emerging evidence for labor pain reduction. Reasonable nonpharmacologic next step for primary dysmenorrhea, with evidence favoring high-frequency over low-frequency TENS.

_comment: EBM notes pushed from the PHCEP app for AI conversion to 衛教資源. Each entry must have: id, date, content (≥4 non-empty lines). The convert-ebm-to-edu workflow processes this file and clears it after success.
